data.title: Key facts: Vodafone aims for 50% revenue growth; share buyback done; ex-dividend on Nov 20
data.description:
• Vodafone Group plans to boost business customer revenue from 25-30% to around 50% by offering AI and cybersecurity services, focusing on small- and medium-sized enterprises.1
  • On November 20, 2025, Vodafone Group Plc completed a share buyback with Merrill Lynch, acquiring shares as part of a program started on November 11, 2025.2
  • Vodafone Group will trade ex-dividend on November 20, 2025, meaning shareholders will not be entitled to the latest dividend pay-out on this date.3

data.title: Key facts: Unilever mulls sale of Marmite, Colman's, Bovril brands; retains Pot Noodle
data.description:
• Unilever is considering selling its British brands Marmite, Colman's, and Bovril to focus on its beauty and wellness sectors.1
  • Unilever will keep its Pot Noodle brand while focusing on its top 30 brands in Europe, aiming to divest from some food brands, potentially worth 1 billion euros in sales.2

data.title: Key facts: HSBC merges trading units; CEO of Australia and NZ resigns; Oman hosts Economist Roadshow
data.description:
• HSBC is merging its trading units to create a 'financing powerhouse,' enhancing client connections and operational efficiency, according to a memo from Patrick George, the bank's global head.1
  • Antony Shaw will step down as CEO of HSBC Australia and New Zealand at year-end. Steve Hughes, current head of wholesale banking, will take over on January 1, 2026.2
  • HSBC Oman hosted the first Economist Roadshow series in Muscat in over two years, where HSBC's economists discussed macroeconomic trends and the investment landscape in Oman and the GCC region.3

data.title: Key facts: Guinea launches Simandou Project; Rio Tinto stock rises 2.3%
data.description:
• On November 12, 2025, Guinea launched the Simandou Project, a key iron ore deposit, with Rio Tinto in a consortium with Baowu and Chinalco to manage the project.1
  • Chinese steel mills are now using Rio Tinto's Pilbara Blend Fines, leading to a 40% drop in inventory to 6.5 million tons as of November 18.2
  • Rio Tinto's stock rose 2.3% as Australian mining shares climbed, fueled by a two-week high in iron ore prices and a rebound in the basic materials sector.3

data.title: Key facts: Barclays PLC buys back 248.6M shares; regulatory update issued; Faruqui extends secondment
data.description:
• On November 20, 2025, Barclays PLC bought back 248.6 million shares at an average price of 380.56p, reducing its issued share capital to 13.9 billion shares.1
  • On November 20, 2025, Barclays PLC announced a regulatory update regarding Petershill Partners PLC, as part of its compliance and engagement review.2
  • Omar Faruqui has extended his secondment as Director General from Barclays until 31 July 2026, as confirmed by the Takeover Panel on 20 November 2025.3

data.title: Key facts: BP closes Olympic Pipeline due to leak; share buyback ongoing; arbitration outcome favorable
data.description:
• BP has closed the 400-mile Olympic Pipeline due to a leak near Everett, Washington, halting fuel delivery. Excavation operations for pipeline inspection are ongoing.1
  • On November 20, 2025, BP bought 1,513,810 shares in a buyback program, bringing total treasury shares to 827,684,368 out of 15,658,628,626 issued shares.2
  • BP recently achieved a favorable outcome in an arbitration case, which has been referenced as a positive precedent by Orlen's CEO in their ongoing arbitration against Venture Global.3

data.title: Key facts: AstraZeneca's Koselugo gains FDA approval; stock rises 1.4%
data.description:
• AstraZeneca's Alexion unit gained FDA approval for Koselugo, now treating adults with neurofibromatosis type 1 and inoperable plexiform neurofibromas, boosting its market presence.123
  • AstraZeneca's stock rose by 1% following the approval of its drug aimed at controlling the growth of non-cancerous tumors in the U.S., with an additional 0.4% increase noted in subsequent trading.4

data.title: Australia's Accent Group hits 2020 low on dour earnings forecast
data.description:
** Shares of Accent Group fall as much as 18.8% to A$0.975, their lowest level since April 24, 2020

** Footwear company heading for worst day since late August 22, if losses hold

** Co sees H1 EBIT between A$55 million and A$60 million ($35.48 million and $38.70 million) vs EBIT of A$80.7 million last year

** Sees full-year EBIT between A$85 million and A$95 million vs A$110.4 million last year

** For first 20 weeks of FY26, like for like retail sales down 0.4% vs VA consensus estimate of 2.15% growth for 1H26

** Co says retail market conditions remain challenging, including ongoing promotional activity

** Citi "surprised" by magnitude of weakness in the trading update

** Stock down ~53% this year, including the day's move

($1 = 1.5504 Australian dollars)

data.title: Tom Lee speculates wounded market makers behind crypto crunch
data.description:
The recent downward pressure on the cryptocurrency market could be the result of deep holes in the balance sheets of market makers, according to Tom Lee, chairman of Ether treasury company BitMine.

Speaking with CNBC on Thursday, Lee suggested that the Oct. 10 market crash, which saw a record $20 billion liquidated from the market, ultimately caught some market makers off-guard, causing severe liquidity issues.

With less capital to operate, combined with reduced capital from traders as their primary source of revenue, it’s a tough time for market makers, Lee said. As a result, this has also led them to shrink their “balance sheet further” in a bid to free up more capital.

“And if they’ve got a hole in their balance sheet that they need to raise capital, they need to reflexively reduce their balance sheet, reduce trading. And if prices fall, they’ve got to then do more selling. So I think that this drip that’s been taking place for the last few weeks in crypto reflects this market maker crippling,” he said.

Lee, who is also the co-founder of Fundstrat, likened the importance of crypto market makers to “central banks” and suggested that the market may face more pain for a few more weeks until the market makers’ liquidity issues are resolved.

“Today’s stock market looks a lot like an echo of what happened on October 10th. But on October 10th, that liquidation was so big [...] it really crippled market makers,” he said, adding:

“And market makers are critical in crypto because they provide liquidity. I mean, they act almost as the central bank in crypto.”

Bitcoin (BTC) was priced at over $121,000 before the Oct. 10 crash, and has since declined back to $86,900, with most of the market following a similar pattern.

Lee said there may be another couple of weeks of market maker unwinding before the market starts to heal, as he pointed to a similar occurrence from 2022: 

“And so in 2022, it took eight weeks for that to really get flushed out. We’re only six weeks into it. So I kind of concur. I think crypto, Bitcoin and Ethereum are in some ways a leading indicator for equities because of that unwind. And now this sort of limping and weakened liquidity.”

data.title: Class Action Filing Alert for Molina Healthcare, Inc. (MOH): Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC Advises Shareholders With Major Losses to Take Action
data.description:
NEW ORLEANS, LA, LA / ACCESS Newswire / November 20, 2025 / Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC ("KSF") and KSF partner, former Attorney General of Louisiana, Charles C. Foti, Jr., remind investors that they have until December 2, 2025 to file lead plaintiff applications in a securities class action lawsuit against Molina Healthcare, Inc. ("Molina" or the "Company") , if they purchased or otherwise acquired the Company's securities between February 5, 2025 and July 23, 2025, inclusive (the "Class Period"). This action is pending in the United States District Court for the Central District of California.

What You May Do

If you purchased securities of Molina and would like to discuss your legal rights and how this case might affect you and your right to recover for your economic loss, you may, without obligation or cost to you, contact KSF Managing Partner Lewis Kahn toll-free at 1-877-515-1850 or via email (lewis.kahn@ksfcounsel.com), or visit https://www.ksfcounsel.com/cases/nyse-moh/ to learn more. If you wish to serve as a lead plaintiff in this class action, you must petition the Court by December 2, 2025.

About the Lawsuit

Molina and certain of its executives are charged with failing to disclose material information during the Class Period, violating federal securities laws.

On July 23, 2025, the Company reported its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30, 2025 and cut its full-year 2025 earnings guidance, disclosing that "GAAP net income was $4.75 per diluted share for the second quarter of 2025, a decrease of 8% year over year" and it "now expects its full year 2025 adjusted earnings to be no less than $19.00 per diluted share," due to a "challenging medical cost trend environment," including "utilization of behavioral health, pharmacy, and inpatient and outpatient services."

On this news, the price of Molina's shares fell $32.03, or 16.84%, to close at $158.22 per share on July 24, 2025, on unusually heavy trading volume.

The case is Hindlemann v. Molina Healthcare, Inc., et al., No. 2:25-cv-09461.

About Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC

KSF, whose partners include former Louisiana Attorney General Charles C. Foti, Jr., is one of the nation's premier boutique securities litigation law firms. This past year, KSF was ranked by SCAS among the top 10 firms nationally based upon total settlement value. KSF serves a variety of clients, including public and private institutional investors, and retail investors - in seeking recoveries for investment losses emanating from corporate fraud or malfeasance by publicly traded companies. KSF has offices in New York, Delaware, California, Louisiana, Chicago, New Jersey, and a representative office in Luxembourg.

TOP 10 Plaintiff Law Firms - According to ISS Securities Class Action Services

To learn more about KSF, you may visit www.ksfcounsel.com.

Contact:

Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC

Lewis Kahn, Managing Partner

lewis.kahn@ksfcounsel.com

1-877-515-1850

1100 Poydras St., Suite 960

New Orleans, LA 70163

data.title: A Chicago Neighborhood With a Suburban Vibe Is Seeing Home Prices Soar — WSJ
data.description:
By Alina Dizik | Photography by Katrina Wittkamp for WSJ

For Chicago families craving additional space, a tree-lined community with an easy commute to downtown is the new hot spot.

It isn't Lincoln Park or Lakeview or even Bucktown. It's Lincoln Square.

Chicagoans are jockeying for single-family homes in this north-side neighborhood, where there is plenty of room for backyards and multicar garages. Luxury homes in Lincoln Square sold for a median of almost $2.3 million in the 12 months through October, up 38% from three years ago, according to an analysis by Juliana Yeager, a Chicago real-estate agent who co-runs Yeager Team.

Buyers are drawn to Lincoln Square for the kind of homes more likely to exist in the suburbs, and they can get those wider floor plans here with the bonus of a commuter train stop.

Chicagoans Carolyn and Chris Ohlgart didn't want to move out of the city, but with a 4-year-old and a baby on the way at the time, they were eager for outdoor space. The compromise: a 3,800-square-foot, classic Chicago-style two-flat home with a grassy yard on the edge of Lincoln Square. The couple, both 37, bought the 1912 property, already renovated into a single-family home, in May for $1.87 million.

"We didn't want to be in the suburbs, but liked some aspects of suburban life," Carolyn says. They now have space for a gym and two offices, making it easier to work from home, and Chris, a lawyer, can easily get downtown to his office. From Lincoln Square, the commuter train takes 18 minutes and two stops.

Lincoln Square, which includes the Ravenswood area, is the kind of lower-density, walkable enclave where kids play outside and neighbors socialize. Large parks include baseball fields, numerous playgrounds and flowering prairie fields, and nearby private and public schools draw in young families. North Lincoln Avenue includes indie shops, a deli and a cobblestone plaza with a fountain. The community hosts annual events like the Square Roots music festival and an Oktoberfest, celebrating the area's German heritage.

The neighborhood feel was a big draw for Susan Aurinko, a 74-year-old artist and designer, and her husband, Gary Mostow, 70, who moved from the West Loop, a buzzy area close to downtown. Aurinko now helps organize the annual block party and is part of her block's book club.

"It's like a small town in the middle of the city," she says.

The couple bought their roughly 8,850-square-foot lot in 2017 for $1.2 million, tore down the existing house and spent another $1.2 million constructing a new home that they moved into in 2020. They had space for an elevator, golf simulator, gym, screening room and prep kitchen for easier entertaining. They have a backyard garden and landscaped front yard. "I wanted trees, I wanted birds and I wanted the sound of children, " says Aurinko.

But a real gem is the detached, three-car garage that allows Mostow to rotate his car collection and shield the vehicles from the Chicago winters.

Lincoln Square has become even more popular since the pandemic gave homeowners a renewed appreciation for outdoor space, says Yeager, the local agent. The area's wider and deeper lots allow for yards and three-car garages, which are more difficult to find in neighborhoods like Lincoln Park that are closer to downtown. In Lincoln Square, large, three-level, single-family homes have front porches and cozy stoops. Many are less than 20 years old with modern amenities.

Luxury single-family homes are in demand on Chicago's north side. Prices have risen the fastest in Lincoln Square, though Lincoln Park remains the more expensive neighborhood.

Many of the new-construction homes in Lincoln Square are more than 5,000 square feet with two floors and a basement, cost north of $2.5 million and sit on 5,000-square-foot lots, Yeager adds.

Many buyers in Lincoln Square come from areas south, including Lincoln Park, Lakeview and Bucktown, says Marta Landrosh, 44, a real-estate agent who also runs the home-building company Landrosh with her husband, Patrick Landrosh, 57.

"It's a quiet pocket," she says of Lincoln Square. "They want the ability to spread out."

The Landroshes' roughly 6,600-square-foot home, built in 2019, is listed privately there for $4.2 million. Marta says they are building a new house two doors down with a larger yard; they previously lived in another home on the block.

For Alexandra and Donnie Fitzgarrald, it wasn't just the extra space for their family that drew them away from the popular Lakeview area in 2021. It was the sense of community.

In Lincoln Square, Alexandra, 43, and Donnie, 54, purchased a six-bedroom, contemporary home for $3.35 million. They have a trampoline in the yard for their now 12- and 10-year-old kids and a back deck off the kitchen, plus a garage and office space.

When their daughter hosted a lemonade stand with a neighbor friend, Alexandra sent out a quick text to the neighbors. "Three-quarters of the block showed up to get lemonade," she says.

The shares are owned indirectly and jointly by Gregory Wasson and Kimberly Wasson, who hold voting and dispositive power over the securities.

data.title: Palomar Holdings Executives Sell Shares to Cover Tax Obligations
data.description:
Reporter NameRelationshipTypeAmountSEC Filing
Christianson JonPresident, 10% OwnerSell$67,254Form 4
Uchida T ChristopherChief Financial Officer, 10% OwnerSell$100,752Form 4
Knutzen JonathanChief Risk Officer, 10% OwnerSell$36,204Form 4

Several executives at Palomar Holdings have recently sold shares of the company's stock, primarily to cover tax obligations related to the vesting of Restricted Stock Units (RSUs).

Jon Christianson, President and 10% Owner, sold 522 shares at a price of $128.84 per share, totaling $67,254. Following this transaction, Christianson directly owns 59,631 shares.

T Christopher Uchida, Chief Financial Officer and a 10% owner, sold 782 shares at the same price, totaling $100,752. Post-sale, Uchida directly owns 7,916 shares.

Jonathan Knutzen, Chief Risk Officer and 10% Owner, also participated in similar transactions, selling 281 shares at $128.84 each for a total of $36,204. Knutzen's direct ownership stands at 21,565 shares following the sale.

data.title: Australia's Worley falls after Jefferies cuts PT on heavier earnings skew to second half
data.description:
** Shares of Worley fall as much as 3.1% to A$12.81, set for fourth straight session of declines

** Jefferies cuts price target after the engineering services provider flagged a larger-than-expected earnings skew to the second half

** Brokerage cuts PT to A$15.50/shr from A$16.18/shr; retains "buy" rating

** Co reaffirmed guidance for full-year on Thursday, but said earnings might be more skewed to the second half than prior years due to project cancellations and restructuring costs

** Jefferies cuts FY26 and FY27 EBITA estimates by 1% and 3%, respectively, reflecting broader macro weakness and impact from restructuring costs

** Jefferies sees some risks into FY27, given weaker European sustainability-led activity and generally weaker macro conditions

** Stock down 4.9% YTD, including the day's moves

data.title: Japanese Shares Follow Wall Street Lower
data.description:
The Nikkei 225 Index fell 2.4% to below 48,700 while the Topix Index slipped 0.5% to 3,282 on Friday, reversing the prior session’s gains and tracking another selloff on Wall Street overnight.

US stocks weakened as worries about an AI bubble persisted and stronger-than-expected US jobs data reinforced expectations that the Federal Reserve will hold off on cutting interest rates in December.

Tech and AI-linked names led the decline in Japan, with SoftBank Group, Advantest, Kioxia Holdings, Fujikura and Lasertec losing between 3.8% and 14%.

Investors also positioned ahead of a widely expected stimulus announcement from Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s government, which has put pressure on the yen and domestic bonds.

On the data front, Japan’s core inflation quickened to a three-month high in October, while exports topped expectations.
